source_paper_title: Stochastic Subgradient Descent Escapes Active Strict Saddles on Weakly Convex Functions
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Bianchi_2024, title={Stochastic Subgradient Descent Escapes Active Strict Saddles on Weakly Convex Functions}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2021.0194}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2021.0194}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Bianchi, Pascal and Hachem, Walid and Schechtman, Sholom}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1761–1790} }
context:
Section 5.2 (Further Topics), pages 14–16; see also Remark 4 on pages 16–17.

An important but challenging problem is to establish, in the absence of weak convexity, that both active strict saddles and sharply repulsive points are avoided.

Thus, the question of the generic convergence of SGD toward local minimizers remains open in the non-weakly convex setting.
title: Prove generic convergence of stochastic subgradient descent without weak convexity
background_latex:
Let \(f:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}\) be a locally Lipschitz function, and let \(\partial f(x)\) denote its Clarke subdifferential. Consider stochastic subgradient descent (SGD)
\[
 x_{n+1}=x_n-\gamma_n v_n+\gamma_n\eta_{n+1},\qquad v_n\in \partial f(x_n),
\]
where \((\gamma_n)\) is a deterministic step-size sequence with \(\gamma_n\downarrow 0\), \(\sum_n\gamma_n=\infty\), and typically \(\sum_n\gamma_n^2<\infty\). The noise \((\eta_{n})\) is adapted to a filtration \((\mathcal{F}_n)\), satisfies the martingale difference property \(\mathbb{E}[\eta_{n+1}\mid \mathcal{F}_n]=0\), and has bounded conditional moments (e.g., bounded conditional fourth moments).

A point \(x^\star\) is Clarke critical if \(0\in \partial f(x^\star)\). In the definable (o-minimal) setting, Clarke critical points admit additional geometric structure via stratifications, and one can define an \(C^p\) active manifold \(M\) around \(x^\star\) on which \(f\) is smooth and off which the subgradients have norm bounded away from \(0\). An \(\emph{active strict saddle}\) is a Clarke critical point \(x^\star\) lying on such a manifold \(M\) for which the Riemannian Hessian of the restriction \(f\vert_M\) has a negative curvature direction at \(x^\star\). A \(\emph{sharply repulsive critical point}\) (as described in the cited manuscript [37] referenced by the paper) is, generically in the non-weakly convex definable case, a Clarke critical point \(x^\star\) lying on an active manifold \(M\) such that \(x^\star\) is a local minimizer of \(f\vert_M\) while nearby subgradients point toward \(M\) (intuitively attracting iterates toward \(M\) rather than repelling them).

For definable weakly convex functions, generic perturbations yield that every Clarke critical point is either a local minimizer or an active strict saddle, and the paper proves SGD avoids active strict saddles under suitable noise conditions, implying generic convergence to local minimizers in that setting.
problem_definition_latex:
Establish, for \(f\) definable and locally Lipschitz but \(\emph{not assumed weakly convex}\), a generic convergence guarantee for SGD of the form above: namely, prove that under standard Robbins--Monro-type conditions on \((\gamma_n)\) and suitable nondegenerate noise conditions on \((\eta_n)\), the iterates \((x_n)\) converge (when bounded) to a local minimizer of \(f\) with probability one.

Equivalently (as suggested by the paper's generic critical-point classification), show that in the non-weakly convex definable setting SGD \(\emph{avoids}\) with probability one both:
1) active strict saddles, and
2) sharply repulsive critical points,
so that the only possible limiting Clarke critical points are local minimizers.
latex:
Let \(f:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}\) be a locally Lipschitz function, and let \(\partial f(x)\) denote its Clarke subdifferential. Consider stochastic subgradient descent (SGD)
\[

where \((\gamma_n)\) is a deterministic step-size sequence with \(\gamma_n\downarrow 0\), \(\sum_n\gamma_n=\infty\), and typically \(\sum_n\gamma_n^2<\infty\). The noise \((\eta_{n})\) is adapted to a filtration \((\mathcal{F}_n)\), satisfies the martingale difference property \(\mathbb{E}[\eta_{n+1}\mid \mathcal{F}_n]=0\), and has bounded conditional moments (e.g., bounded conditional fourth moments).

An \(\emph{active strict saddle}\) is a Clarke critical point \(x^\star\) lying on such a manifold \(M\) for which the Riemannian Hessian of the restriction \(f\vert_M\) has a negative curvature direction at \(x^\star\). A \(\emph{sharply repulsive critical point}\) (as described in the cited manuscript [37] referenced by the paper) is, generically in the non-weakly convex definable case, a Clarke critical point \(x^\star\) lying on an active manifold \(M\) such that \(x^\star\) is a local minimizer of \(f\vert_M\) while nearby subgradients point toward \(M\) (intuitively attracting iterates toward \(M\) rather than repelling them).

Establish, for \(f\) definable and locally Lipschitz but \(\emph{not assumed weakly convex}\), a generic convergence guarantee for SGD of the form above: namely, prove that under standard Robbins--Monro-type conditions on \((\gamma_n)\) and suitable nondegenerate noise conditions on \((\eta_n)\), the iterates \((x_n)\) converge (when bounded) to a local minimizer of \(f\) with probability one.

Equivalently (as suggested by the paper's generic critical-point classification), show that in the non-weakly convex definable setting SGD \(\emph{avoids}\) with probability one both:
1) active strict saddles, and
2) sharply repulsive critical points,
so that the only possible limiting Clarke critical points are local minimizers.
location_in_paper: Section 5.2 (Further Topics), pages 14–16; see also Remark 4 on pages 16–17.
open_problem_quote:
An important but challenging problem is to establish, in the absence of weak convexity, that both active strict saddles and sharply repulsive points are avoided.

Thus, the question of the generic convergence of SGD toward local minimizers remains open in the non-weakly convex setting.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can algorithmically search for low-dimensional definable, locally Lipschitz non-weakly convex examples (e.g., piecewise polynomial/absolute-value constructions) where SGD with specified noise does or does not converge to non-minimizing critical points, but faithfully testing generic (almost-everywhere perturbation) statements and asymptotic avoidance is computationally delicate and depends on long-run behavior.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate functions and noise models can be parameterized (e.g., piecewise-defined definable functions) and evaluated via simulation-based proxies such as empirical frequency of convergence to candidate non-minimizers; however, the true statement is asymptotic and generic, so the fitness signal is indirect and may be noisy, making evolutionary search helpful for proposing counterexamples or conjectural conditions but unlikely to fully resolve the question.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Bianchi--Hachem--Schechtman, 2024) establishes that for definable weakly convex locally Lipschitz objectives, SGD with Robbins--Monro stepsizes and sufficiently nondegenerate martingale noise avoids convergence to any active strict saddle. The proof hinges on two geometric ingredients around an active manifold \(M\): (i) a Verdier-stratification-based strengthened projection formula controlling how Clarke subgradients project onto tangent spaces of adjacent strata, and (ii) an angle (proximal aiming) condition ensuring iterates contract toward \(M\). Once iterates are shown to track the projected dynamics \(y_n=P_M(x_n)\), the method reduces to a smooth stochastic approximation on \(M\) and invokes a Brandi\`ere--Duflo/Pemantle-type trap-avoidance argument in the negative-curvature directions of the Riemannian Hessian of \(f|_M\).

Beyond weak convexity, the same paper explains why the generic landscape changes: in addition to local minimizers and active strict saddles, one generically encounters "sharply repulsive critical points" (local minimizers of \(f|_M\) for which nearby subgradients point toward \(M\)), and it explicitly states that generic convergence of SGD to local minimizers remains open in this regime (Section 5.2 and Remark 4). The most directly relevant forward-citing work is the thesis "Saddle Avoidance, Asymptotic Normality, and Exponential Acceleration in Nonsmooth Optimization", which appears to remove weak convexity in the saddle-avoidance part by developing generic regularity conditions for definable/semi-algebraic objectives with active manifolds and proving almost-sure avoidance of active strict saddles for stochastic subgradient-type methods. However, based on the provided citation analysis, it is not confirmed that this thesis fully resolves the additional obstruction posed by sharply repulsive critical points in the non-weakly convex definable setting; it may instead work in a generic Clarke-regular class where such points are excluded or controlled.

Several other forward citations contribute tools toward a full resolution. "On the diameter of subgradient sequences in o-minimal structures" gives deterministic sequential convergence of bounded Clarke-subgradient iterates with \(\alpha_k\asymp 1/k\) to a Clarke critical point in the non-weakly-convex definable setting, which could serve as a key compactness/oscillation-control component in a stochastic approximation proof. "Sufficient conditions for instability of the subgradient method with constant step size" provides geometric instability criteria near semi-algebraic critical manifolds, conceptually aligned with sharply repulsive behavior, suggesting a route to show such points are not attractors for (stochastic) subgradient dynamics. Finally, works on stratification-based shadowing/rates and on Lyapunov stability for Euler discretizations of differential inclusions offer complementary frameworks for analyzing attraction/repulsion near strata, potentially enabling a unified argument that stochastic perturbations prevent convergence to both negative-curvature saddles on \(M\) and to sharply repulsive Clarke critical points when weak convexity is absent.

sources.title: Saddle Avoidance, Asymptotic Normality, and Exponential Acceleration in Nonsmooth Optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: The thesis develops regularity conditions (notably strong-(a) and (b≤) regularity) for locally Lipschitz, Clarke-regular semialgebraic/definable functions with active manifolds, and uses them to prove saddle avoidance for stochastic or randomly perturbed subgradient methods: under Robbins–Monro stepsizes and nondegenerate perturbations/noise, the stochastic subgradient method (and projected/proximal variants) almost surely does not converge to an active strict saddle of a generic semialgebraic objective; consequently, any almost sure limit point (when the iterates converge/bounded) must be a local minimizer for generic Clarke-regular semialgebraic problems satisfying the strict-saddle-type classification (local minimizers vs active strict saddles).
sources.connection:
This is directly aimed at the open problem: it studies SGD/subgradient methods on definable (semialgebraic) locally Lipschitz functions without assuming weak convexity, using active-manifold geometry. It proves almost-sure avoidance of active strict saddles for stochastic subgradient-type algorithms under noise/perturbations, and establishes generic regularity along active manifolds that yields an “aiming toward the manifold” property and a shadow-iteration argument.

However, the open problem also asks to rule out convergence to sharply repulsive critical points (a phenomenon specific to non-weakly convex definable functions). From the provided excerpt, the thesis claims convergence only to minimizers for “generic, Clarke regular semialgebraic problems,” but it is unclear whether it explicitly defines/shows avoidance of sharply repulsive critical points in the sense of the cited manuscript [37] or whether it restricts to a generic class where such points do not occur (e.g., via Clarke regularity/strict-saddle classification). Thus, based on the available pages, the thesis appears to give substantial partial progress—removing weak convexity for saddle avoidance and giving generic convergence-to-minimizers statements under additional regularity/genericity hypotheses—but I cannot confirm it fully resolves the sharply-repulsive-critical-point obstruction in complete generality for all definable locally Lipschitz functions.

sources.title: On the diameter of subgradient sequences in o-minimal structures
sources.theorem_or_result:
For a locally Lipschitz function \(f:\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R\) definable in a polynomially bounded o-minimal structure, the paper proves a quantitative diameter bound for any (deterministic) Clarke-subgradient sequence \(x_{k+1}\in x_k-\alpha_k\partial f(x_k)\) staying in a compact set and near a level set: there exist \(\alpha,\beta,\epsilon,\varsigma_1,\varsigma_2>0\) and \(\theta\in(0,1)\) such that if \(0<\alpha_{K-1}\le\cdots\le\alpha_0\le\bar\alpha\) and \(x_{0:K}\subset X\cap\{|f|\le\epsilon\}\), then
\[
\operatorname{diam}(\{x_0,\dots,x_K\})\le \varsigma_1\big(\operatorname{sgn}(f(x_0))|f(x_0)|^{1-\theta}-\operatorname{sgn}(f(x_K))|f(x_K)|^{1-\theta}\big)+\varsigma_2\Big(\alpha_0^\beta+\sum_{k=0}^{K-1}\alpha_k^{1+\beta}+\big(\sum_{k=0}^{K-1}\alpha_k^{1+\beta}\big)^{1-\theta}+\sum_{k=0}^{K-1}\alpha_k\big(\sum_{j=k}^{K-1}\alpha_j^{1+\beta}\big)^\theta\Big).
\]
In particular, any bounded subgradient sequence with decreasing stepsizes \(\alpha_k\asymp 1/(k+1)\) converges to a Clarke critical point (Corollary 1.2).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for a generic almost-sure convergence guarantee for *stochastic* subgradient descent on definable locally Lipschitz functions without weak convexity, i.e., to show (when bounded) convergence to local minimizers by avoiding both active strict saddles and sharply repulsive critical points.

This paper does not study stochastic noise, saddle avoidance, or genericity of limiting points. Instead it establishes deterministic convergence of bounded subgradient sequences under stepsizes \(\alpha_k\asymp 1/k\) (and more generally provides a diameter estimate controlled by function-value variation plus higher-order stepsize sums). This is relevant because it supplies a strong *convergence-to-a-critical-point* mechanism in the non-weakly-convex definable setting, potentially usable as a building block in a stochastic approximation analysis (e.g., to show that once the stochastic iterates track a deterministic subgradient sequence, oscillations are ruled out). However, it does not address the key missing piece of the open problem: ruling out convergence to non-minimizing critical points (active strict saddles or sharply repulsive points) under nondegenerate noise.

sources.title: Sufficient conditions for instability of the subgradient method with constant step size
sources.theorem_or_result: Theorem 1 (Josz–Lai, 2023): For a locally Lipschitz semi-algebraic function \(f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}\) with critical set \(S:=\{x:0\in\partial f(x)\}\), assume \(S\) is a \(C^2\) manifold at \(x^*\in S\) of dimension \(<n\). Assume there exist \(\theta_1\ge 0\), a neighborhood \(U\) of \(x^*\), and a continuous function \(C\) such that for any constant stepsize \(\alpha>0\) there is \(c_1>0\) with \(C(x_{k+1})-C(x_k)\ge c_1 d(x_k,S)^{\theta_1}\) for every subgradient-method trajectory \((x_k)\subset U\setminus S\) with stepsize \(\alpha\). Then \(x^*\) is strongly unstable for the constant-stepsize subgradient method if either (i) \(\theta_1=0\), or (ii) \(\partial f\) is metrically \(\theta_2\)-subregular at \((x^*,0)\) with \(\theta_2>1\) and \(f\) satisfies the Verdier condition at \(x^*\) along \(S\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for almost-sure convergence of diminishing-stepsize stochastic subgradient descent on definable locally Lipschitz (not weakly convex) functions, equivalently avoidance of active strict saddles and sharply repulsive critical points. This paper does not analyze diminishing-stepsize SGD or almost-sure convergence; instead it gives deterministic instability criteria for the constant-stepsize (set-valued) subgradient method near certain (non-strict) local minima of semi-algebraic functions. Conceptually, it is relevant because it identifies geometric conditions (metric \(\theta\)-subregularity with exponent \(>1\), Verdier condition, and existence of a Chetaev function) under which a local minimum is repelling for subgradient iterations—phenomena akin to “sharply repulsive” critical points in the non-weakly convex definable setting. However, the result is local, deterministic, and for constant stepsize, so it does not establish the requested generic convergence/avoidance for Robbins–Monro stepsizes with martingale noise.

sources.title: On convergence rates of subgradient descent on semialgebraic functions
sources.theorem_or_result:
Under geometric assumptions implied by Lipschitz stratifications (hence holding for semialgebraic functions and more generally definable functions in polynomially bounded o-minimal structures), constant-step subgradient descent iterates locally shadow a Riemannian gradient descent on an appropriately selected active stratum, and one can assemble descent inequalities across successive strata to obtain explicit finite-time convergence rates to a stationarity measure based on 
- the Riemannian gradient norms \(\|\nabla_{X} f(x_k)\|\) on active strata, and 
- a global projection formula relating Clarke subgradients to these Riemannian gradients.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for almost-sure convergence of stochastic subgradient descent (RobbinsMonro steps, martingale noise) to local minimizers for definable locally Lipschitz functions without weak convexity, equivalently showing avoidance of both active strict saddles and sharply repulsive critical points.

This paper studies (primarily deterministic) subgradient descent, focusing on finite-time complexity/rates under stratification-based geometric conditions (global projection formula, curvature bounds, tubular neighborhood estimates) that hold for semialgebraic/definable functions. These results provide structural tools (shadowing of Riemannian gradient descent on strata; mechanism to handle stratum switching) that could be useful in analyzing stochastic dynamics near active manifolds, including near sharply repulsive points. However, from the provided excerpt it does not establish the required stochastic avoidance results or almost-sure convergence to local minimizers in the non-weakly convex definable setting.

sources.title: Riemannian stochastic optimization methods avoid strict saddle points
sources.theorem_or_result: For a smooth objective function \(f\in C^2\) on a geodesically complete Riemannian manifold \(M\), consider the Riemannian Robbins–Monro (RRM) scheme \(X_{n+1}=\exp_{X_n}(\gamma_n \hat v_n)\) with surrogate gradients \(\hat v_n=v(X_n)+U_n+b_n\), where \(v=-\mathrm{grad}\,f\). Under: (i) \(v\) geodesically \(L\)-Lipschitz; (ii) stepsizes \(\sum_n\gamma_n=\infty\) and \(\sum_n \lambda^{1/\gamma_n}<\infty\) for all \(\lambda\in(0,1)\); (iii) bias \(\|b_n\|\le C\gamma_n\), bounded noise \(\|U_n\|\le \sigma\), and a uniform excitability condition \(\mathbb E[[\langle U_n,z\rangle]^+\mid \mathcal F_n]\ge \zeta\) for all unit \(z\in T_{X_n}M\); and (iv) injectivity radius bounded below; then for any strict saddle manifold \(S\) of \(f\), \[\mathbb P(\mathrm{dist}(X_n,S)\to 0)=0.\] (Theorem 1).
sources.connection:
The open problem concerns Euclidean stochastic subgradient descent for locally Lipschitz, definable (o-minimal) functions without weak convexity, and asks for generic convergence to local minimizers by avoiding both active strict saddles and sharply repulsive Clarke critical points. This paper proves an avoidance result, but in a different regime: \(f\) is \(C^2\) (smooth), gradients (not Clarke subgradients) are used, and the dynamics live on a Riemannian manifold with exponential/retraction updates. It establishes that Riemannian stochastic approximation avoids strict saddles (including saddle submanifolds) under nondegenerate noise.

Thus, it does not address nonsmooth Clarke-critical phenomena (active manifolds, sharp repulsion) nor the non-weakly-convex definable setting. However, its methodology—APT coupling to gradient flow, stable/center manifold arguments, and noise “excitability” conditions—could be conceptually relevant if one could extend it to differential inclusions / nonsmooth stratified dynamics arising from Clarke subgradients on definable sets.

sources.title: Lyapunov stability of the Euler method
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper develops Lyapunov-type criteria for stability of Euler discretizations of differential inclusions. In particular, Theorem 1.2: if (a) a set-valued map \(F\) is upper semicontinuous with nonempty compact values, (b) there exists a locally Lipschitz potential \(f\) of a conservative field \(D\) such that locally \(\max_{u\in \operatorname{co}F(x)}\min_{v\in D(x)}\langle u,v\rangle\le -\min_{w\in D(x)}\kappa(w)\) for some positive definite \(\kappa\), and \(f(x)\) is an isolated \(D\)-critical value, (c) there is a continuous function \(g\) that is d-Lyapunov near \(x\), and (d) \(x\) is a strict local minimizer of \(f+g\), then \(x\) is d-stable for the Euler scheme \(x_{k+1}\in x_k+\alpha_k F(x_k)\) with sufficiently small variable stepsizes. Extensions (Theorems 3.13, 3.15) give asymptotic \(p\)-d-stability/attractor results under \(\sum \alpha_k^p=\infty\), \(\alpha_k\to 0\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a generic almost-sure convergence guarantee for stochastic subgradient descent on definable locally Lipschitz functions without weak convexity, i.e., show SGD avoids both active strict saddles and sharply repulsive critical points. This paper does not analyze stochastic iterates, definability/genericity, or avoidance of unstable critical points. Instead it provides deterministic stability and (under additional \(p\)-d-Lyapunov decrease) asymptotic attraction results for Euler discretizations of differential inclusions, with an application to the Bouligand subgradient method for semi-algebraic \(f\). These Lyapunov/d-Lyapunov techniques could be a tool for proving stability/attraction of certain sets of minima (including nonisolated/flat minima) for deterministic subgradient-like methods, but they do not address the key stochastic avoidance mechanisms needed to rule out sharply repulsive critical points or strict saddles in the non-weakly convex definable setting.

sources.title: Contributions to non-convex stochastic optimization and reinforcement learning
sources.theorem_or_result: The thesis establishes (for continuously differentiable nonconvex objectives) almost sure convergence of several stochastic momentum/adaptive-gradient algorithms with Robbins–Monro stepsizes to the set of critical points, and proves an avoidance-of-traps theorem for non-autonomous stochastic approximation schemes: for recursions \(z_{n+1}=z_n+\gamma_{n+1} b(z_n,\tau_n)+\gamma_{n+1}\eta_{n+1}+\gamma_{n+1}\rho_{n+1}\) with \(\sum\gamma_n=\infty\), \(\sum\gamma_n^2<\infty\), martingale-difference noise \(\eta_{n+1}\), and a linearization \(b(z,t)=D(z-z^\star)+e(z,t)\) near an equilibrium \(z^\star\) where \(D\) has an eigenvalue with positive real part, the event \(\{z_n\to z^\star\}\) has probability 0 under a nondegeneracy (“exciting noise”) condition in the unstable directions. Applied to smooth \(F\), this yields nonconvergence to strict saddles/local maxima for the considered algorithms.
sources.connection: The open problem concerns stochastic subgradient descent for locally Lipschitz definable (possibly non-weakly convex) functions and asks for generic convergence to local minimizers by avoiding both active strict saddles and sharply repulsive critical points (a nonsmooth phenomenon tied to active manifolds/Clarke subdifferentials). This thesis works almost entirely in the \(C^1\) setting (stochastic gradients, Hessians) and its trap-avoidance result targets unstable equilibria of an underlying (non-autonomous) ODE, corresponding to smooth strict saddles/maxima. It does not address Clarke criticality, active manifolds, definability stratifications, or sharply repulsive critical points, nor does it treat nonsmooth subgradient dynamics without weak convexity. At best, it provides a methodological template (non-autonomous avoidance-of-traps via invariant manifolds) that might be adaptable to the smooth dynamics on an active manifold, but the key missing piece is handling attraction to manifolds and nonsmooth normal dynamics that create sharply repulsive points.
